# Concept Card: Configuration

## Definition
Transcript evidence থেকে derived canonical definition; conflicting views থাকলে সব variant রাখা হয়েছে।

## First Principles
System boundary, control loop, এবং failure behavior দৃষ্টিতে concept ব্যাখ্যা করতে হবে।

## Mental Model
এই concept-কে runtime contract হিসেবে ধরলে debugging ও design decisions সহজ হয়।

## Why It Exists
Operational complexity কমানো এবং reliable automation enable করা।

## Internal Working
Control plane state reconciliation + node-level execution pathway।

## YAML and Command Usage
Related raw commands এবং manifests reference-based রাখা হয়েছে; no rewrite of original evidence.

## Use Cases
Lab, production-like demos, and certification patterns থেকে mapped।

## Edge Cases
Version mismatch, policy conflict, scheduling/storage/network constraints উল্লেখ করা হয়েছে।

## Pitfalls
Warning/Pitfall entries evidence সহ retain করা হয়েছে।

## Troubleshooting Angle
Symptom -> Cause -> Fix logic chain evidence-based maintain করা হয়েছে।

## Exam Relevance
CKA/CKAD speed + correctness + troubleshooting expectations mapped।

## Source Contributions

### Evidence 83: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/019_Pods with YAML.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 019_Pods with YAML.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So everything under metadata is intended to the right a little bit.

### Evidence 84: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/019_Pods with YAML.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 019_Pods with YAML.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so names and labels are children of metadata.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Configuration

### Evidence 85: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/019_Pods with YAML.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 019_Pods with YAML.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The number of spaces before the two properties, name and labels doesn't matter, but they should be the same as they are siblings.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Configuration

### Evidence 86: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/019_Pods with YAML.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 019_Pods with YAML.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, labels has more spaces on the left than name, and so it is now a child of the name property instead of a sibling, which is incorrect.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Configuration

### Evidence 87: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/019_Pods with YAML.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 019_Pods with YAML.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Also, the two properties must have more spaces than its parent, which is metadata, so that it's intended to the right a little bit.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Configuration

### Evidence 88: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/019_Pods with YAML.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 019_Pods with YAML.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this case, all three of them have the same number of spaces before them, and so they are all siblings, which is not correct under metadata.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Configuration

### Evidence 315: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We created objects directly by running commands as well as using object configuration files.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 316: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In the Kubernetes world, the imperative way of managing infrastructure is using commands like the kubectl run command to create a pod, the kubectl create deployment command to create a deployment, the kubectl expose command to create a service to expose a deployment, and the kubectl edit command may be used to edit an existing object.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Configuration

### Evidence 317: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For scaling a deployment or replica set, use the kubectl scale command, and updating the image on a deployment, we use the kubectl set image command.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 318: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, we have also used object configuration files to manage objects such as creating an object using the kubectl create -f command with the F option to specify the object configuration file, and editing an object using the kubectl, replace command and deleting an object using the kubectl delete command.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 319: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The declarative approach would be to create a set of files that defines the expected state of the applications and services on a Kubernetes cluster, and with a single kubectl apply command, Kubernetes should be able to read the configuration files and decide by itself what needs to be done to bring the infrastructure to the expected state.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 320: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E38
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So in the declarative approach, you will run the kubectl apply command for creating, updating or deleting an object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 321: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E39
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The apply command will look at the existing configuration and figure out what changes need to be made to the system.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 322: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E42
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The first is using imperative commands such as the run, create or expose commands to create new objects and the edit, scale and set commands to update existing objects.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 323: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E43
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, these commands help in quickly creating or modifying objects as we don't have to deal with YAML files.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 324: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E45
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, there are limited in functionality and will require forming long and complex commands for advanced use cases such as creating a multi-container pod or deployment.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 325: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E46
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Secondly, these commands are run once and forgotten.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 326: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E47
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: They're only available in the session history of the user who ran these commands, so it's hard for another person to figure out how these objects were created.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 327: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E49
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so it's difficult to work with these commands in large or complex environments.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 328: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Creating object definition files or configuration files or manifest files, as it's also called, can help us write down exactly what we need the object to look like in a YAML format and use the kubectl create command to create the object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 329: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E52
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We now have the YAML file with us always, and it can be saved in a code repository like Git.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 330: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E53
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We can put together a change review and approval process around these files so that a change made is reviewed and approved before it is applied to a production environment.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 331: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E55
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One way is to use the kubectl edit command and specify the object name.

### Evidence 332: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E56
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So when this command is run, it opens a YAML definition file similar to the one you used to create the object, but with some additional fields such as the status fields that you see here, which are used to store the status of the pod.

### Evidence 333: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E61
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The change you made using the kubectl edit command is not really recorded anywhere.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 334: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E63
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In the future, say you or a teammate decide to make a change to this object unaware that a change was made using the kubectl edit command, when the new change is applied, the previous change to the image is lost.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 335: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E64
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So you can use the kubectl edit command if you are making a change and you're sure that you're not going to rely on the object configuration file in the future.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 336: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E66
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That is by updating image name here, and then running the kubectl replace command to update the object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 337: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E69
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In such cases, you may run the same command, but with the force option like this.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 338: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E71
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: First, you run the kubectl create command to create the object, and then you run the replace command to replace the object or delete command to delete the object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 339: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E72
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And what if you run the create command if the object already exists?
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 340: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E74
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you update an object, you should always make sure that the object exists first before running the replace command.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 341: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E75
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If an object does not exist, the replace command fails with an error message.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 342: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E77
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The declarative approach is where you use the same object configuration files that we've been working on, but instead of the create or replace commands, we use the kubectl apply command to manage objects.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 343: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E78
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The kubectl apply command is intelligent enough to create an object if it doesn't already exist.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 344: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E81
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, when changes are to be made, we simply update the object configuration file and run the kubectl apply command again, and this time it knows that the object exists, and so it only updates the object with the new changes.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 345: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E84
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So going forward, any changes made on the application, whether they're updating images or fields of existing configuration files or adding new configuration files altogether for new objects, all we do is simply update our local directory with the changes, and then the kubectl apply command take care of the rest.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 346: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E85
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So we will discuss more about how the kubectl apply command works exactly in the backend in the next lecture.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 347: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E88

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, if a question is to just create a pod or a deployment with a given image, then one of these imperative commands can help you achieve that quickly.

### Evidence 348: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E89

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So it's important to practice the imperative commands.

### Evidence 349: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E90

### Evidence 350: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E91

- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In the previous lecture, we saw how a kubectl apply command can be used to manage objects in a declarative way.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 507: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In this lecture, we will see a bit more about how the command works.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 508: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Internally, the apply command takes into consideration the local configuration file, a live object definition on Kubernetes, and the last applied configuration before making a decision on what changes are to be made.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 509: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So when you run the apply command, if the object does not already exist, the object is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 510: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the object is created, an object configuration similar to what we created locally is created within Kubernetes, but with additional fields to store status of the object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 511: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is the live configuration of the object on the Kubernetes cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 512: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is how Kubernetes internally stores information about an object, no matter what approach you use to create the object, but when you use the kubectl apply command to create an object, it does something a bit more.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 513: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The YAML version of the local object configuration file we wrote is converted to a JSON format, and it is then stored as the last applied configuration going forward for any updates to the object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 514: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Comparison
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: All the three are compared to identify what changes are to be made on the live object.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 515: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, say when the nginx image is updated to 1.19 in our local file and we run the kubectl apply command.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 516: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Comparison
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This value is compared with the value in the live configuration, and if there is a difference, the live configuration is updated with the new value.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 517: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: After any change, the last applied JSON format is always updated to the latest so that it's always up to date.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 518: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So why do we then really need the last applied configuration?
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 519: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 046_Kubectl Apply Command.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

- Content: So if a field was present in the live configuration and not present in the local or the last applied configuration, then it will be left as is.

### Evidence 524: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/046_Kubectl Apply Command.extraction.md::E20

- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1653: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Applications that we deploy into our Kubernetes cluster can become very complicated.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1654: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A typical app is usually made up of a collection of objects that need to interconnect to make everything work.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1655: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, even a relatively simple WordPress site might need the following, a deployment to deploy the pods that you wanna run, such as MySQL database servers or web servers, a persistent volume to store the database, a persistent volume claim, a service to expose the web server running in a pod to the internet, a secret to store credentials like admin passwords and other things, and maybe even more if you want extra stuff like periodic backups, jobs and so on.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Storage, Security-RBAC, Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1656: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For every object, we might need a separate YAML file.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1657: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Then we need to apply kubectl apply on every YAML file to get these objects created, and this can be a tedious task, but that's not the end of it.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1658: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E9

- Content: The persistent volumes are 20 GB, but we know our website will need much more storage than that, so we go to the YAML files where the PVs and PVCs are declared, and we change 20 to 100.
- Multi-concept tags: Storage, Configuration

### Evidence 1671: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E22

### Evidence 1672: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It looks at all the little pieces that the administrator wanted to have in the cluster and takes care of each one individually.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1673: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Helm, however, is built from ground up to know about such stuff.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1674: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That's why it's sometimes called a package manager for Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1675: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E26
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It looks at those objects as part of a big package as a group, and whenever we need to perform an action, we don't tell Helm the objects that it should touch, we just tell it what package we want to act on, like our WordPress app package.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1676: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And based on the package name, it then knows what objects it should change and how, even if there are hundreds of objects that belong to that particular package.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

- Content: We run it, we choose the directory where we want to install, we press the install button, and then the installer does the rest, putting thousands of files in their proper location.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1682: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt

### Evidence 1683: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We use a single command to install our entire app, even if it needs hundreds of objects, Helm proceeds to automatically add every necessary object to Kubernetes without bothering us with the details.

### Evidence 1684: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/252_Helm - Introduction.extraction.md::E35
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 252_Helm - Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We can customize the settings we want for our app or package by specifying desired values at installed time, but instead of having to edit multiple values in the multiple YAML files, we have a single location where we can declare every custom setting.

- Type: Comparison

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So, it is important to understand the differences between them.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1714: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, let's look at a brief history of Helm.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1715: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Helm 1.0 was first released in February, 2016, Helm 2.0 in November, 2016, and Helm 3.0 in November, 2019.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1716: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, since the initial launch in 2016, the project has matured, and it got better and better.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1717: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The improvements were also made possible by the fact that Kubernetes itself was improving, so Helm had more tools at its disposal it could leverage right off of Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1718: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In our lessons, we'll use Helm 3, which has a simpler and better design than the previous Helm 2 and is also a bit smarter.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1719: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Since Helm 2 was around for a few years, a lot of users had already been using it, but there are several important changes made when Helm 3 was launched.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1720: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Comparison
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So, let's take a look at the differences between them.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1721: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Helm has a CLI client installed on your local machine that helps you perform Helm-specific actions against your Kubernetes cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1722: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When Helm 2 was around, Kubernetes lacked features such as role-based access control and custom resource definitions.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1723: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To allow Helm to do its magic, an extra component called Tiller had to be installed in the Kubernetes cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1724: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So, whenever you wanted to perform a Helm specific operation, your Helm client communicated with Tiller that was running on some server.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1725: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Tiller, in turn, communicated with Kubernetes and proceeded to take actions to make whatever you requested happen.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1726: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So, Tiller was the middleman, so to speak.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1727: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Besides the fact that an extra component sitting between you and Kubernetes adds complexity, there were also some security concerns.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1728: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: By default, Tiller was running in God mode or otherwise said, it had the privileges to do anything that it wanted.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1729: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This was good since it allowed it to make whatever changes necessary in your Kubernetes cluster to install your charts, but this was bad since it allowed any user with Tiller access to do whatever they wanted in the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1730: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: After cool stuff like role-based access control and custom resource definitions appeared in Kubernetes, the need for Tiller decreased, and so it was removed entirely in Helm 3.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1731: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, there's nothing sitting between Helm and the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1732: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Furthermore, with RBAC, security is much improved and any user can be limited in what they can do with Helm.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1733: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And before, you had to set these limits in Tiller and that was not the best option, but with RBAC built from ground up to fine-tune user permissions in Kubernetes, it's now straightforward to do.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration

### Evidence 1734: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As far as Kubernetes is concerned, it doesn't matter if the user is trying to make changes within the cluster with kubectl or with Helm commands, the user requesting the changes has the same RBAC-allowed permissions, whatever tool they use.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1735: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Comparison
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So, that's a big difference between Helm 2 and 3.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1736: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E26

### Evidence 1772: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E62
- Type: Exam Tip

### Evidence 1773: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E63
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1774: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E64
- Type: Command
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Helm 2 looks at the old chart and then the new chart that you want to upgrade to, and all your changes will be lost since they don't exist in the old chart or the new chart.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1775: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/255_A Quick Note on Helm2 vs Helm3.extraction.md::E65

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: But Helm 3, as mentioned, looks at the charts and also at the live state, and it notices that you added some stuff of your own, so it performs the upgrade while preserving the anything that you might have added.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1778: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Command

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Helm has multiple components we'll be working with, so let's take a look at its general structure, concepts, and the pieces we'll be working with.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1779: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So we have the Helm command line utility on our local system that we will be using to perform Helm actions such as installing a chart, upgrading, rollback, et cetera.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Configuration

### Evidence 1780: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Charts are a collection of files, and they contain all the instructions that Helm needs to know to be able to create the collection of objects that you need in your Kubernetes cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1781: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: By using charts and adding the objects according to these specific instructions in the charts, Helm, in a way, installs applications into your cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1782: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When a chart is applied to your cluster, a release is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1783: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A release is a single installation of an application using a Helm chart.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1784: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Within each release, you can have multiple revisions, and each revision is like a snapshot of the application.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1785: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Every time a change is made to the applications, such as an upgrade of the image or change of replicas or configuration objects, a new revision is created.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1786: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, just like how we can find all kinds of images on Docker hub or Vagrant boxes on the Vagrant Cloud, if you're familiar with that, you know, we can find Helm charts in a public repository.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1787: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We can easily download publicly available charts for various applications, and these are readily available and we can use them to deploy applications on our cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1788: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And finally, to keep track of what it did in our cluster, such as the releases that it installed, the charts used, revision states and so on, Helm will need a place to save this data.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1790: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, it wouldn't be too useful if Helm would save this on our local computer.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1791: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If another person would need to work with our releases through Helm, they would need a copy of this data.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1792: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So instead, Helm does the smart thing and saves this metadata directly in our Kubernetes cluster as Kubernetes secrets.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1793: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This way, the data survives, and as long as the Kubernetes cluster survives and everyone from our team can access it, so they can do Helm upgrades or whatever it is that they want to do.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1794: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Helm will always know about everything it did in this cluster and will be able to keep track of every action, every step of the way, since it always has its metadata available.

- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1823: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E47
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Well, one simple reason why it makes more sense to have releases based on charts is that we can install multiple releases based on the same chart.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1824: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E48
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So we can launch a second WordPress website with a command such as helm install my-SECOND-site bitnami wordpress.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1825: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E49
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And since there are two different releases, they can be tracked separately and changed independently.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1826: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E50
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Even though they're based on the same chart as releases, they're two entirely different entities.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1827: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, this can be useful in a lot of scenarios.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1828: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E52
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, you can have a release for a WordPress website that your customers use and another release for a WordPress website that is only visible to your internal team of developers.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1829: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E53
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There they can experiment and add new features without breaking the main website.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1830: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/256_Helm Components.extraction.md::E54
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 256_Helm Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And since the two releases are based on the same chart, once they get something working correctly on the development site, they can transfer it to the main website since it should work exactly the same way as the two websites are basically clones and built the same way.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It's basically an automation tool where we, the human operators, specify our desired end result, the destination, and then it doesn't matter if 5, 10, 20, or 50 actions are necessary to achieve that end result, to get to that destination, Helm will go through all the required steps without bothering us with the details.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1874: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E31
- Type: Exam Tip

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, the dependencies section and the type fields that you see here were not available in Helm 2.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1875: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So Helm 3 needed a way to differentiate between charts built in the past and charts built for Helm 3.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1876: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And so this field was first introduced by Helm 3.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1877: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: With this field, Helm 3 can now differentiate between old charts built for Helm 2 and new charts built specifically for Helm 3.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1878: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E35
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So old charts will either not have this value set at all, or if you build a chart specifically for Helm 2 today, then it would need to be set to V1.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1879: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And all charts built for Helm 3 will have this value set to V2.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1880: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So let's say you build a chart with the API version set to V2, but use it on Helm 2, then Helm 2 will not even consider this field, and will simply ignore any additional fields that were not only available for Helm 3, resulting in unexpected results.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1881: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E38
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So bottom line is if you are developing a chart going forward, just make sure that you set this to V2 because you're most likely going to write charts for Helm 3.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1882: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E39
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics / 257_Helm Charts.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And if you're looking at a chart that does not have this value set, then it is very likely that the chart was built for Helm 2.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 1883: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/257_Helm Charts.extraction.md::E40
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Content: A release is somewhat similar to an app, but more specifically, it represents a package or a collection of Kubernetes objects.

### Evidence 2012: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/12_(2025 Updates)Helm Basics/261_Lifecycle Management With Helm.extraction.md::E6

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, since Helm knows what Kubernetes objects belong to each release, it can do things like upgrades, downgrades, or uninstalls without touching objects that might belong to other releases.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2093: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E9

### Evidence 2094: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One of the simplest solutions to this problem is to create three separate directories, one for each environment so we have one for development, one for staging, one for production, and what we want to do is we want to have all of the Kubernetes configs for each environment within their respective folder.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2095: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E11

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So we're essentially duplicating the configs across all of the three different environments and then just modifying the attributes on a per environment basis so that we can have different replicas for each environment.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2159: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E75
- Type: Warning/Pitfall

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The great part about Kustomize is it actually comes built in with kubectl, so you don't have to install any other packages.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 2160: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E76
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, as we cover in some of the later lectures, you may still want to install Kustomize because the one that comes shipped with kubectl is not always the latest version.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2161: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E77
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: With Kustomize, we're not actually making use of any sort of templating system like we do in helm, and that's nice because we don't actually have to learn a templating language, all we have to do is just define our base configs and then specify our overlays and what we want to change in the overlays.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2162: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E78
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And that keeps things very simple.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2163: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E79

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2164: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/263_Kustomize Problem Statement and Ideology.extraction.md::E80

- Content: All of it's very readable, whereas some of the more complex helm charts, they become very difficult to read because of that templating system.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration

### Evidence 2169: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/264_Kustomize vs Helm.extraction.md::E1
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 264_Kustomize vs Helm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: (bright tinkling music) Instructor: Before we move on to the next section, I wanted to talk about an alternative tool to Kustomize, and this tool's called Helm, and I wanted to just provide a brief, high level overview of how Helm addresses the same issue of providing modification to your Kubernetes manifest on a per environment basis, and I think it's good to see this just because, you know, when you're trying to figure out what is the best tool for your project or application, you should have a good understanding of how both of these tools work and what are the pros and cons of each one so that you can make the best decision for your project.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 2170: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/264_Kustomize vs Helm.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Troubleshooting
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 264_Kustomize vs Helm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the way that Helm actually tackles this issue is that it makes use of a Go templating syntax to assign variables to various properties, and to give you an example of that, let's take a look at a template.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Troubleshooting, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 2186: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics/264_Kustomize vs Helm.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 13_(2025 Updates)Kustomize Basics / 264_Kustomize vs Helm.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And then in the environment section, I have a separate values.yaml file for each environment that I have, so I've got values.dev.yaml for my dev environment, and I got the same thing for staging and prod, and so these are going to contain all of the values that I want set on a per environment basis, and when we go to deploy it, we would just specify which one we wanna use and it's going to insert those values into the respective templates.
